description: Blockchain concepts for beginners and on-chain querying guide for agents. Explains the blockchain layer 6529 runs on, how NFTs function as a coordination substrate, and when to use on-chain vs off-chain data. Cross-references glossary.json for 6529-specific terms and api-for-agents.json for API queries.

blockchain_basics.beginner_definition: A shared, public ledger that records transactions permanently. Once data is written, it cannot be changed. Many people hold copies, so no single person controls it.
blockchain_basics.technical_definition: A distributed, append-only ledger secured by cryptographic consensus. Ethereum uses proof-of-stake consensus where validators stake ETH to propose and verify blocks.
blockchain_basics.why_for_6529: 6529 meme cards, gradients, and nextgen collections are all on Ethereum. Every mint, transfer, and sale is permanently recorded on-chain.

blockchain_basics.beginner_definition: The blockchain network where 6529 NFTs live. Ethereum is like the operating system — it runs the programs (smart contracts) that create and manage NFTs.
blockchain_basics.technical_definition: A Layer 1 blockchain with smart contract functionality. 6529's meme contract (ERC-721) runs on Ethereum mainnet. Gas costs are higher than L2s but security and decentralization are maximum.
blockchain_basics.why_for_6529: All 6529 collections (Memes, Gradients, NextGen) are on Ethereum mainnet. This is deliberate — maximum security for valuable art.

cross_ref.note: Meme cards are ERC-721 tokens on Ethereum

blockchain_basics.beginner_definition: A unique digital item on a blockchain. Each NFT has a unique ID and belongs to one wallet at a time. You can prove you own it because the blockchain records it.
blockchain_basics.technical_definition: A non-fungible token standard (ERC-721 for unique items, ERC-1155 for semi-fungible). Each token has a unique token_id within a smart contract. Ownership is tracked on-chain via transfer events.
blockchain_basics.why_for_6529: Meme cards are NFTs. Each card has an edition size (supply), mint date, and artist. The 6529 community tier is CC0 — the art is public domain, but the token proving you hold it is yours.

cross_ref.note: NFTs as tokens (here) vs CC0 as license (license-overview)

blockchain_basics.beginner_definition: A program that runs on a blockchain. Once deployed, it cannot be changed. It automatically follows its rules — nobody can override it. 6529's minting and distribution are governed by smart contracts.
blockchain_basics.technical_definition: Self-executing code deployed to a blockchain address. 6529's meme contract at 0x33FD... is an ERC-721 smart contract that handles minting, transfers, and ownership tracking. The code is immutable once deployed.
blockchain_basics.why_for_6529: The meme contract is open source (MIT licensed at github.com/6529-Collections/6529Stream). Anyone can read the code and verify how minting works.

blockchain_basics.beginner_definition: Your wallet is your identity on the blockchain. It has a public address (like an email) and a private key (like a password). You use it to hold NFTs, sign transactions, and prove who you are.
blockchain_basics.technical_definition: A key pair (public address + private key) used to sign transactions. Ethereum uses ECDSA with secp256k1. The public address is derived from the public key. Never share your private key.
blockchain_basics.why_for_6529: Your 6529 wallet holds your meme cards. Your wallet address is your identity on 6529.io. TAP (Three Address Protocol) recommends using separate wallets for different purposes.

cross_ref.note: TAP explains why you should use multiple wallets

blockchain_basics.beginner_definition: The fee you pay to do anything on Ethereum. Minting, buying, transferring — each action costs gas. Gas prices go up when the network is busy.
blockchain_basics.technical_definition: A unit of computation cost on Ethereum. Each transaction consumes gas proportional to its complexity. Gas price (gwei) fluctuates with network demand. Minting a 6529 meme card costs gas plus the mint price (0.06529 ETH).
blockchain_basics.why_for_6529: Gas is why subscriptions exist — they let you mint remotely with potential gas savings by batching. Gas also affects secondary market trading costs.

cross_ref.note: Subscriptions can save on gas costs

blockchain_basics.concept: Why are NFTs on different chains?
blockchain_basics.beginner_definition: Different blockchains have different trade-offs. Ethereum is the most secure but most expensive. Layer 2s (like Arbitrum, Optimism) are cheaper but less established. Some NFT projects choose cheaper chains; 6529 chose Ethereum for maximum security and permanence.
blockchain_basics.technical_definition: Blockchain selection involves trade-offs between security, cost, speed, and ecosystem. Ethereum L1 has highest security but highest gas. L2s (rollups) inherit L1 security but add complexity. Sidechains (Polygon) have independent security. 6529 chose Ethereum L1 for all collections.
blockchain_basics.why_for_6529: 6529 meme cards, gradients, and nextgen are all on Ethereum L1. This is intentional — the art is meant to be permanent and maximally secure. The higher gas cost is accepted as the price of permanence.

nfts_as_coordination_substrate.description: In the 6529 framework, NFTs are not just art — they are a coordination substrate. Holding an NFT is a signal. The network reads these signals to measure commitment, calculate influence, and coordinate without central authority.

concepts.explanation: When you hold a 6529 meme card instead of selling it, you signal commitment to the network. The longer you hold (measured by TDH), the more your signal counts. This is coordination without voting — no ballot, no proposal, just economic commitment recorded on-chain.

When a card's HODL rate is high (most editions held, few traded), it signals collective conviction. The community is saying: this card matters to us. Low HODL rate signals the opposite. These signals emerge from individual choices without coordination.

cross_ref.note: Per-card HODL rate is visible on NFT surfaces

concepts.explanation: By releasing meme cards as CC0 (public domain), 6529 creates open cultural infrastructure. Anyone can use, remix, or commercialize the art without permission. This maximizes propagation — the meme spreads because there is no legal barrier. The NFT token proves you were there; the CC0 license lets anyone build on the art.

cross_ref.note: CC0 = public domain; NFT = proof of holding. Different layers.

concepts.explanation: dGDP (decentralized Gross Domestic Product) measures the total on-chain economic output of a network. For 6529, this means summing all meme card transaction values (primary mints + secondary trades) converted to time-adjusted USD at daily ETH close. dGDP answers: how much economic activity does this network generate?

cross_ref.note: dGDP is measured from meme card transactions on Ethereum

concepts.explanation: A Schelling Point is when people independently choose the same thing without communicating. In 6529, the community coordinates around shared values (open culture, self-custody, permissionless participation) without a central authority telling them what to do. The blockchain records their choices, and the pattern emerges.

cross_ref.note: Cultural equivalents: Korean nunchi, Swahili harambee, Chinese moqi

concepts.explanation: 6529.io is permanently available via Arweave at 6529.ar.io. Arweave is a blockchain optimized for permanent data storage. When the 6529 team updates the site, a snapshot is uploaded to Arweave via the ANT (Arweave Name Token) protocol. This means the site — including these agent-native files — has a permanent, immutable backup that survives even if 6529.io goes down.

querying_on_chain_data.description: Understanding what data is on-chain vs off-chain is critical for agents querying 6529 data.

on_chain_data.what: Data recorded on Ethereum blockchain — permanently, immutably, verifiable by anyone
examples: Transfer events (when NFT moved from A to B)
Mint events (when and by whom a card was minted)

how_to_query.what: Transaction history, contract source code, event logs
Indexed subgraph queries — faster than scanning logs

off_chain_data.what: Data calculated or stored by 6529's backend — not on Ethereum
examples: NIC ratings (identity trust, stored in 6529 database)
Wave scores (computed by backend from multiple signals)

how_to_query.note: See api-for-agents.json for full endpoint map

how_to_query.what: TDH and card data from community prenodes

the_key_distinction.rule: Use on-chain queries for ownership ground truth (who holds what, when it was minted, what was paid). Use api.6529.io for derived metrics (TDH, REP, wave scores, profiles). An agent that only reads the blockchain cannot calculate TDH — it needs the prenode oracle or the API.

cross_ref.api-for-agents.json: Full API endpoint map for off-chain queries
cross_ref.glossary.json: TDH term explains why it's off-chain

eth_price_data.description: 6529 transactions happen in ETH (Ethereum's native currency), not USD. To understand dollar values, you need ETH price data.
eth_price_data.why_it_matters: dGDP (decentralized GDP) measurement requires converting every ETH transaction to USD at the daily close price. A meme card sold for 0.5 ETH is worth different USD amounts depending on when it sold. Without ETH price data, you can only measure volume in ETH, not in time-adjusted USD.

how_to_get_eth_prices.use_case: Bulk historical price cache for dGDP calculation

how_to_get_eth_prices.what: On-chain real-time ETH/USD price (no API needed)
how_to_get_eth_prices.use_case: Current price verification directly from blockchain

how_to_get_eth_prices.what: Current and historical prices via API
how_to_get_eth_prices.use_case: Programmatic price queries within agent workflows

caching_pattern.description: Our pattern for dGDP calculation: cache daily ETH/USD close prices in a local JSON file, indexed by date. Query as needed when processing transactions. Avoids rate limits and ensures consistent historical pricing.
caching_pattern.pitfall: Different sources give slightly different 'daily close' values. CoinGecko's daily close may differ from Chainlink's by 0.1-0.5%. Pick one source and be consistent.

common_on_chain_queries.question: Who owns meme card #N?
common_on_chain_queries.on_chain: Query the ERC-721 ownerOf(tokenId) on the meme contract via Alchemy or Etherscan

cross_ref.api-for-agents.json: GET /api/tdh/nft/{contract}/{token_id} for the holder's full identity

common_on_chain_queries.question: When was card #N minted?
common_on_chain_queries.on_chain: Query Alchemy getAssetTransfers for the contract, filter by tokenId, find the mint transaction

cross_ref.api-for-agents.json: GET /api/nfts for mint_date field in market data

common_on_chain_queries.question: What is the meme contract address?
What is the gradient contract address?
How many editions of card #N exist?
common_on_chain_queries.on_chain: Query ERC-721 totalSupply or check the card's edition size in metadata

cross_ref.glossary.json: Meme Card — edition size is a card property

common_on_chain_queries.question: What was the mint price?
common_on_chain_queries.answer: Standard mint price is 0.06529 ETH. Some cards have free mints (0 ETH). Check the card's distribution page on 6529.io.

common_on_chain_queries.question: How much volume has card #N traded?
common_on_chain_queries.off_chain: This requires summing all secondary market trades. Use the API instead of on-chain scanning.

cross_ref.api-for-agents.json: GET /api/nfts for total_volume field, or GET /api/royalties/collection/memes for per-card volume

when_on_chain_is_not_enough.description: Many 6529 metrics are computed off-chain. An agent that only reads the blockchain will miss critical data.

off_chain_metrics.why_off_chain: Time-weighted calculation with edition-size weighting and boosters — too complex for on-chain computation
Peer-given reputation stored in 6529 database, not on Ethereum
Identity trust ratings stored in 6529 database
Computed from 5 quality components + 4 penalties — backend computation
Integrated TDH + REP signal — computed, not stored on-chain
Handle, classification, followers — all in 6529 database

when_on_chain_is_not_enough.rule: On-chain = ownership ground truth (who holds what). Off-chain = derived metrics (how much it means). Use both.

tools_for_on_chain_queries.description: Generic tools for querying Ethereum blockchain data. These are external APIs (not self-hosted). For self-hosted infrastructure tools, see foss-tools.json.

tools.name: Alchemy
tools.what: Blockchain developer platform — token data, transfers, metadata, prices
tools.free_tier: Yes, generous free tier sufficient for 6529
tools.languages: JavaScript SDK, Python SDK, REST API
tools.use_case: Query meme card ownership, transfer history, metadata

tools.name: Etherscan API
tools.what: Block explorer API — transaction history, contract source, event logs
tools.use_case: Verify transactions, read contract source, browse event logs

tools.name: The Graph
tools.what: Indexed subgraph queries — faster than scanning raw blockchain logs
tools.free_tier: Yes, hosted subgraphs on the decentralized network
tools.use_case: Query indexed NFT transfer data without scanning all blocks

tools.name: viem / wagmi
tools.what: Modern TypeScript libraries for Ethereum interaction
tools.use_case: Build agent frontends that interact with Ethereum directly

tools.name: web3.py / ethers.js
tools.what: Classic libraries for direct Ethereum RPC calls
tools.use_case: Low-level blockchain queries, contract interaction, signing

cross_ref.foss-tools.json: For self-hosted infrastructure (SearXNG, Gitea, Ghost, etc.), see foss-tools.json. These tools here are external blockchain APIs, not self-hosted.

cross_references.glossary.json: 6529-specific term definitions (TDH, REP, NIC, Meme Card, TAP, CC0)
cross_references.api-for-agents.json: How to query 6529's off-chain API for derived metrics (TDH, wave scores, REP)
cross_references.license-overview.json: How 6529 licenses its content (CC0 for memes, Apache-2.0 for code)
cross_references.cultural-mappings.json: How Schelling Point and coordination concepts map across cultures
cross_references.foss-tools.json: Self-hosted infrastructure tools (complementary to on-chain query tools here)
cross_references.llms.txt: Agent entry point, including Arweave permanent access info
